Activate and Focus. Pulse discreetly starts vibrating every 30 seconds, every minute or every 5th minute.

A universe of the best teachers
The first smart wearable that doesn't stress you with data and information.
Mindfulness and wellbeing only
Pulse vibrations are completely separate from smartphone or other notifications.
Unintrusive and discreet
It's simply the most subtle smart wearable ever.
Smart without phone
Unlike regular meditation apps, it is made for digital detox.

core.v: A mindfulness ring that brings you back to the present — anti-data, nearly screen-free, nothing to check. The one screen that exists is deliberate, not ambient.
Pulse is a smooth gold ring that knows nothing about you — on purpose. A few times an hour it taps your finger. You look up. Here you are. No screen, no scores, nothing to check, ever.

Every other ring reports on your life. Pulse gives you the afternoon back — it taps, and the afternoon is still going on. Every buzz you've ever felt wanted something from you; this one wants nothing. A ping summons. A pulse hands you the room.

Stop watching the movie of your mind. Start making the movie of your life — the same footage, at the speed of being here. It isn't only the phone: even with empty hands, you can be replaying, rehearsing, worrying, instead of living what's actually in front of you.

Lead with restraint and an unconditional return, never arithmetic. Spine: "Wear it a month. If it isn't for you, send it back — no reason needed, no questions." The guarantee grades nothing: asking a wearer to certify presence would reinstall the scorecard the ring refuses. Frame the $200 as paying for restraint, not utility — "The one thing you'll ever own that keeps no score of you," with "for the cost of one nice dinner" as a quiet frame beneath it, never an argument. Keep ROI and reclaimable-hours math out of the hero; that number stays downstream and gentle, never a grade. Proof = real wearers' anonymous before/after words — the only number we'd trust, so the only one we sell.

Pulse is a verb. The ring sends a vibration; the Pulse is the ritual of presence that follows. The ritual can look like breath, focus, feeling fully, appreciation, intimacy, or creativity — surrender, honesty and courage are held back pending advisory-board review (persona research flagged them as Seeker-specific, at risk of overreach in mass copy). Rate this to weigh in.

There are only three reasons to look down at a screen with Pulse: to set your intention, to deepen your practice, or to find the others. Every other reason, you look up. The vocabulary stays pulses, never pings — you schedule your pulses the way you schedule a practice, not the way you dread a notification.
Numb → Seen → Hopeful → Convinced → Relieved → Grateful → Generous.
Present tense, second person, one breath per sentence. Invite, never instruct. No exclamation points.
When would now be a good time? · Be Here WOW · Here & Now Together · Three reasons to look down. Every other reason, you look up.

hierarchy.label: First
hierarchy.text: the invitation: 'When would now be a good time?' pinned to the plain line beneath it, 'A gold ring that knows nothing about you — on purpose.' The koan leads; the plain line names the object (gold ring) and the wedge (knows nothing) so a distracted stranger gets it cold in three seconds. Nothing to prove above the fold.

hierarchy.label: Second
hierarchy.text: the wedge as category law: every other ring reports on your life; Pulse gives you the afternoon back — no screen, no score, nothing to check, because your life was never data. This is why the object exists and why it costs $200.

hierarchy.label: Third
hierarchy.text: the concrete return: one moment the body recognizes — the kettle you actually hear, the last page of the story read to the end — and the felt tap itself: 'A small tap on your finger — you look up, and the room is still there.' How little it asks: two minutes to set up, then it just taps; the remembering is yours.

hierarchy.label: Fourth
hierarchy.text: the flag, the objection, and the proof: the brag the wearer repeats — 'It doesn't do anything. That's the point. The one thing I own that keeps no score of me' — and the sayable answer for the first friend who asks 'isn't that just another buzz?': 'A notification wants something from you. This wants nothing — it taps, and the wanting stops.' Concede the haptic resemblance, then flip the errand: a ping summons you toward a task; the pulse hands you the room and asks nothing back. Then real wearers' anonymous before/after words and an unconditional 30-day return that grades nothing.

hierarchy.label: Fifth
hierarchy.text: the founder and the tribe: Johan answers replies himself, usually with coffee; this is for people who'd rather live the moment than measure it — 'I stopped measuring my life to start living it' — and not for the optimizer chasing streaks. The refusal is the flag that makes the right people feel found.

guardrails.text: The wedge is a different verb, not fewer numbers. Never argue on data's ledger — no 'tracks less,' no 'removes a number,' no subtraction framing. They report; we return. They ask how you did; we ask where you are.

The return is a felt thing, not a retrieval. The ring stores nothing and hands nothing back. Say 'the afternoon back' or 'it was there all along,' never 'the ring does the remembering'; the remembering belongs to the wearer.

Land it in the chest, not the head: one felt, particular sensory instant beats any category of 'moments.' Ration each sensory prop (the kettle, the coffee, the tap, the stairs) to one appearance per surface.

Perform presence, don't narrate it. Prefer a present-tense instant that happens in the reader's body — 'You're halfway up the stairs before you notice you took them,' 'You look up. Here you are.' — over a line that reports the benefit from outside the moment.

Kill the 'just another notification' objection by direction-of-ask, never by feature. Concede the haptic resemblance, then flip the errand: a ping summons and wants something; a pulse releases and wants nothing. The winning distinction is 'wants nothing' — never argue it's a better alert, smarter reminder, or quieter buzz; that files us back in the bucket we die in.

The flag is the wearer's to repeat, not ours to shout. Remarkability comes from a first-person owner-voice line the buyer says out loud — steal these verbatim from the corpus so they're earned, not clever.
Never 'you will feel' — always 'you may notice.' The uncertainty is the active ingredient.
Ban the destination verbs: no 'become,' 'get,' 'improve,' 'reduce,' 'unlock,' 'transform,' 'elevate,' 'journey.' Presence is arriving, not a place you arrive at.
Kill 'scientifically proven' and all proof-by-authority. Proof = real wearers' own before/after words only.

Never let the enemy name the product. No 'smart wearable,' no 'notification,' no benefit-bullet lists, no feature/mechanism grid — name the notification only to refuse it, never to describe what Pulse is.
Never ship a score. No quiz score/grade/percentile, no dashboard, no streaks. A number for someone's presence collapses the anti-data soul.
The guarantee grades nothing. The return is unconditional — any reason or none, no questions, no proof of presence at the door.
No shouted transactions. Retire 'SHOP NOW'; the CTA should be as quiet as the pulse. No exclamation points, no urgency or scarcity.
Don't invite the spreadsheet. Keep ROI math and the reclaimable-hours number out of the hero; price as identity and restraint. The dinner line is a quiet frame, not an argument.

The deliverable must withhold like the object. Ship the fewest lines that each wave a flag (~4 taglines, ~7 TOS); hold the rest as an internal palette that lives inside body copy, not on the shipped surface. Cut, don't add; prefer verbatim corpus lines; when in doubt, say less.
Clarity before cleverness in the hero: the koan may lead, but it must sit against one plain line that names the object and the wedge. Never leave the riddle standing alone above the fold.

Speak to the wearer in second person, present tense, and let the sentence embody presence rather than describe it. Keep triple-negation ('counts nothing, grades nothing, remembers nothing') as an owner-voice flag only — never on the shipped surface and never as the wedge's closing argument, where negation drags the reader back into the ledger we left.
Say who it's NOT for. Naming the optimizer and sending them to Oura is what makes the right people feel found.
